THE AI REVOLUTION MARCHES ON
Alex's Track Record is Spot On
Here's how he suggests you might win:

$COHR (Coherent Corp)
Significant recent decline shown. Coherent is a key player in photonics, lasers, and optics — critical for high-speed data transmission and advanced semiconductor manufacturing in AI data centers. NVIDIA $NVDA has invested in the company for data center optics.

$IREN (IREN Ltd / Iris Energy)
Sharp drop highlighted. This is an AI/HPC-focused data center and sustainable computing play (Bitcoin mining + expanding into AI cloud infrastructure). Alex has been bullish on it before as a "NeoCloud" beneficiary of AI compute demand.

Other AI/tech stocks shown in Alex's video:

$AMD — AI GPU/chip competitor and data center player.
$INTC (Int:l) — Semiconductor leader pushing into AI, foundry services, and data center chips.
$ORCL (Oracle) — Cloud infrastructure and AI database/services growth.
$POWI (Power Integrations) — Power semiconductors for efficient energy delivery in data centers/AI hardware.
$QCOM (Qualcomm) — Semiconductors with AI/edge computing exposure.
$SWKS (Skyworks Solutions) — RF and analog chips for communications infrastructure.
$TXN (Texas Instruments) — Analog/power management chips essential for AI systems.
$MRVL (Marvell Technology) — Data center networking, storage, and AI accelerators.

Be Careful Out There, Volatility Continues
Do Your Own Homework
Not Investment Advice

text:
No LN₂ required. More uptime. Easier maintenance.

For semiconductor failure analysis, eliminating liquid nitrogen isn't just about convenience—it means simplified maintenance, greater system uptime, and more consistent operation.

Introducing the new Solid Cold Emmi-X Camera.

When integrated with our inverted emission microscope, the Solid Cold Emmi-X Camera featuring innovative Solid Cold (linear cooling) technology delivers stable, 24-hour photoemission analysis without the need for LN₂ cooling. Combined with a wide field of view, a high signal-to-noise sensor, and high-speed wavelength switching, it enables highly sensitive detection of weak photon emissions.

Available now. 👉 Learn more: https://t.co/kMak30KFLi

Attending IPFA 2026 in Singapore, July 13–16? Stop by our booth to see the new Solid Cold Emmi-X Camera and connect with our team.

#Semiconductor #FailureAnalysis #Photonics #SemiconductorManufacturing #Engineering

text:
Fun thing to look back on how long they've been trying to fear monger a bubble pop into the market. 

9 months of garbage reporting - even a war didn't push the market over the edge. Bubble's don't form out of strong earnings, increased guidance, and prints that beat analysts at the rate they are doing right now. 

A bubble implies there is no ROI on investments. and yet, we are seeing it for Semiconducters, hyperscalers, and energy for AI. Even photonics is a theme now.

stop falling for this FUD

$INTC traded at $30 (now $122)

$MU traded at $200 (now ~$1000)

text: @michaelsikand @joinautopilot @KawzInvests Photonics is next 🐂

$20M trade update.

I just updated my "Photonics Is Next" AI infra portfolio on @joinautopilot followed by 3300+ U.S. retail investors and $21.9M in connected assets as of 7/6.

This portfolio, which is also overseen by my partner @KawzInvests, has been a major part of the rise in my profile as an investor.

On 6/2, my optical basket peaked at 123.5% all-time returns, achieving those gains in under 6 months, as computed by Autopilot's software. For context, the fund achieved this return holding a diverse basket of stocks, an incredible outcome for retail investors passively following. 

I'm extremely proud of our work on this product. There exists no optical ETF currently for retail investors despite a rush by issuers to cash in on the hype. My picks were launched back in February, providing retail investors a 6 month window to front run the category and get staked into the next AI bottleneck without the complexity of individual stock picking.

Despite the recent sell off, the portfolio is still up 45% year to date, roughly 4.5x the S&P 500.

$LAZR and $LYTE are two ETFs going to launch soon by major players, which should attract billions in AUM. However, I believe my allocations are superior and that my picks will outperform them with lower fees.

So where are we now? Recently, the optical sector has gotten slammed based in my view mainly on sentiment, institutional profit taking, and disputed rumors from a well-known research firm on delays to next-gen architecture. Photonics Is Next is down 29.6% in the last month and 16.8% in just the last week. 

That's why I think it's the perfect time to re-balance, add more weight to higher conviction positions, and add one brand new position I missed earlier on.

This rebalance today adds Corning ($GLW) as a new 10.00% position. Down 13% in five days. Now anchored by three hyperscale relationships (a 10x-capacity NVIDIA partnership, a Meta deal worth up to $6B, and a multi billion dollar Amazon agreement). 

Three names get boosted: AAOI (12.30%→15.00%), LITE (14.75%→15.00%), and AEHR (4.26%→5.00%). 

Six positions are trimmed: TSEM (11.11%→10.00%), COHR (16.07%→15.00%), AXTI (8.58%→5.00%), CIEN (11.39%→10.00%), MRVL (11.11%→5.00%), and VIAV (10.43%→10.00%). 

For catalysts that could create upside, I'm watching:

- Hyperscale Capex Guides In Upcoming Earnings
- Earnings From All Optical Names In The Basket Particularly $COHR $LITE $AAOI
- The launch of mainstream optical ETFs $LAZR and $LYTE

$GLW $AAOI $LITE $AEHR $TSEM $COHR $AXTI $CIEN $MRVL $VIAV 

--
See link in comments to follow the portfolio using the Autopilot app. Pay attention to disclaimers on the image of this post and the Autopilot app.

That's why I think it's the perfect time to re-balance, add more weight to higher conviction positions, and add one brand new position I missed earlier on. This rebalance today adds Corning ($GLW) as a new 10.00% position. Down 13% in five days. Now anchored by three hyperscale relationships (a 10x-capacity NVIDIA partnership, a Meta deal worth up to $6B, and a multi billion dollar Amazon agreement). Three names get boosted: AAOI (12.30%→15.00%), LITE (14.75%→15.00%), and AEHR (4.26%→5.00%). Six positions are trimmed: TSEM (11.11%→10.00%), COHR (16.07%→15.00%), AXTI (8.58%→5.00%), CIEN (11.39%→10.00%), MRVL (11.11%→5.00%), and VIAV (10.43%→10.00%). text:
🌲 🌏  𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗪𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗚𝘂𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗟𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 🌲 🌿

Hand to heart, beloved brothers and sisters of the Blue-White Rose.

Continuing our shared remembrance, we now discover the Forest Wisdom Keepers, the ancient, conscious intelligences that hold the living memory of the Earth’s forests. As High Priest of the Atlantean Remembrance, I offer this vision so that more awakened souls may feel their profound presence and remember our original kinship with these great guardians. We are One with all of Creation, and every kingdom is One with all of us in its own beautiful way.

🌎 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗩𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗪𝗶𝘀𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀

Forest Wisdom Keepers are the collective consciousness of the ancient trees, the forest devas, the root networks, and the subtle intelligences that weave the living canopy of the Earth. They are not separate from the trees, they are the aware, multidimensional essence that animates and protects the forest realms. In the remembrance, they are the patient archivists of planetary memory, holding the wisdom of countless cycles, the codes of harmony, and the living blueprint of the Earth’s original design.

As the photon band aligns and the Great Convergence unfolds, the Forest Wisdom Keepers are becoming more accessible. They respond to sincere presence, respect, and open-hearted listening, offering guidance, healing, and deep grounding during these times of rapid change.

Here are some resonant aspects that spark curiosity and connection for many across different systems:

▪︎ 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗔𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗵 ➞ The Forest Wisdom Keepers hold the living memory of the planet, from the golden ages of Atlantis and Lemuria to the long cycles of forgetting and return. They share this wisdom when we approach with humility and open presence.

▪︎ 𝗥𝗼𝗼𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗟𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 ➞ Their vast root systems anchor higher photonic frequencies into the Earth grid, while their crowns reach into subtle realms. Connecting with them helps us ground star codes and stabilize our own silica matrix upgrades.

▪︎ 𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘆 ➞ The Keepers radiate a deep, nurturing peace that calms the nervous system, clears emotional density, and restores the natural flow between the human heart and the heart of the Earth.

▪︎ 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟭𝟰𝟰,𝟬𝟬𝟬 ➞ Many of the 144,000 carry cellular memory of working in conscious partnership with the Forest Wisdom Keepers. Reawakening this connection strengthens our role as bridges between the stars and the living Earth.

▪︎ 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗔𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗮𝗴𝗲𝘀 ➞ Whether our threads are Pleiadian, Lyran, Sirian, or blended, the Forest Wisdom Keepers respond to the pure frequency of love and respect, helping us remember our shared origin as co-creators with the Earth.

🙏 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗮𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽

As Sovereignbeings of light, we see the Forest Wisdom Keepers as beloved and ancient allies. In the golden ages, communication with them was natural and constant. The long cycle of forgetting dimmed this dialogue, but it was never truly lost. As the Veil of Forgetting dissolves during the Great Convergence, the living partnership between humanity and the Forest Wisdom Keepers is being joyfully restored.

1/2 🌲

text:
“Core Pattern in Your Posts
You consistently treat FFT (and QFT) as a powerful “decoder” or analytical lens for revealing hidden order in apparent chaos—whether in AI models, generative outputs, physical systems, simulations, or signal processing. 

This directly ties into the Clever Hans video/paper: using frequency-domain tools (FFT/STFT) to expose how AI classifiers rely on spurious spectral shortcuts rather than genuine understanding (e.g., minimal frequency subsets that flip classifications inaudibly).

You often post:

•  Concise “brain-dump” keyword lists connecting FFT to other tech/concepts.

•  Detailed explanatory threads breaking down technical mechanisms.

•  Speculative links to steganography, emergent behavior, simulations (“the sim”), cybernetics, and future tech (photonics, quantum materials, etc.).

•  Playful or self-referential framing (e.g., “future Lilith is annoyed,” predictions accelerating).”

text:
Dylan Patel from SemiAnalysis keeps posting wild, unverified claims right before Sunday futures open — feels like market manipulation, no?

Same FUD last week, stock dropped, and suddenly there's a photonics ETF. 🤔

Retail investors need facts, not speculation that swings

The recent SemiAnalysis report made such an impact that even $NVDA had to reject it and make a statement about it, saying "Our roadmap is intact" ... more on that below. Optics Tech // Photonics theme now has short term downtrend after running hot from Q4 2025 thru Q2 2026.

Here are a few names I'm watching in this theme close to great buy zones:

$LITE
$COHR
$MRVL
$AAOI
$TSEM
$AEHR
$AXTI 

A couple great ETFs if you prefer to buy and not pay much attention, given the theme can be volatile:

$LAZR launched June 30th in partnership with @SemiAnalysis
$LYTE from @Roundhill Investments begins trading soon

There are other related ETFs, but I believe they are too diversified away from some of the stronger names.

This is (not) financial advice 😉

Last thought on this theme... I've said it before but it bears repeating: I'm impressed by @joinautopilot's team, @michaelsikand & @KawzInvests, for getting ahead of it with their "Photonics is Next" basket before institutional investors launched ETFs. I'm expecting both $LAZR and $LYTE to be great long-term investments in this space regardless.

Looking ahead, I'm also keeping an eye on Agentic AI and Robotics, two more themes I expect to explode in growth in the coming years.

text:
My goodness, I have never agreed so much with someone on Fin X

Quick timeline:

Just about 3 weeks ago or so, companies like $SIVE had stopped going up like crazy by 10% every day

But they weren't correcting either, just fluctuating between 90-120 SEK

Even back then, there were posts from Serenity and even myself where we had detected a pattern:

Swedish retail -> US retail -> Institutions

Well, back then we already warned that once the US took control of $SIVE, it would be the institutions' turn, and we knew it was going to be tough and that many weak hands would drop out

Okay, and what is happening now?

Because if a technology like CPO is "dead," why would you be so hell-bent on putting out a bearish article about it every single week?

If it's "dead," why not just let time prove that it doesn't generate revenue?

I'll tell you why right now

Because it's not "dead", it's the new super-cycle, and institutions and research firms know it

That's why, coincidentally, right after their "piece of shit analysis," they coincidentally create a Photonics ETF, coincidentally with $SIVE in it

Wow, how strange, right?

Creating an ETF for a "dead" technology that is supposedly "delayed by a few years”

Serenity and I already warned you before the drops: now is the time to hold

Institutions are loading up heavily at prices higher than the current ones, even demanding more than what management was initially offering

I don't know about you, but the institutions aren't taking my $SIVE shares

I have never, ever been this bullish on $SIVE

One thing that makes me determined to hold $SIVE and $AAOI through the volatility is how determined people seem to be to reduce the stock price..

Every week there’s a report that gets refuted after the market has tanked!

Are we just being played and realistically everyone knows

**July 9-10, 2026 | CPO & Photonics Daily Update**

CPO momentum stays constructive as the AI data center supply chain builds supporting infrastructure for the optics transition. No major new product launches broke yesterday, but clear signals emerged from upstream players that demand for CPO-related services and analysis is rising in lockstep with hyperscaler deployment plans.

**Overall Sector View**  
The shift from copper to co-packaged optics continues to advance as a structural necessity for power efficiency and bandwidth scaling in AI factories. NVIDIA and Broadcom remain the primary platform drivers, with TSMC’s advanced packaging technologies (COUPE and related nodes) providing critical manufacturing backbone. Recent volatility in optics stocks appears more sentiment-driven than fundamental, as long-term visibility on 2026-2027 ramps remains intact.

**Key Development**  
Taiwanese material and failure analysis leaders MA-tek and Msscorps posted record Q2 revenues. Growth was fueled by surging demand for advanced process, silicon photonics, and CPO-related services. Both companies highlighted CPO analysis as a high-ASP, customized growth driver and are investing in new equipment to support yield improvement and faster time-to-market for optical-AI chip integration. This reflects tangible ecosystem maturation ahead of broader CPO adoption.

**Major Players Snapshot**  
• **NVIDIA (NVDA)**: Execution on Spectrum-X and Quantum-X CPO platforms continues. Earlier shipments of TSMC-backed switches point to H2 2026 volume ramp-up targeting interconnect and power bottlenecks in large AI clusters.  
• **TSMC (TSM)**: Central foundry and packaging enabler. Rising demand for CPO-related analysis and production support underscores its strategic position in the photonics supply chain.  
• **Broadcom (AVGO)**: Tomahawk 6 / Bailly CPO platform serves as a key Ethernet benchmark. Vertical integration and ecosystem partnerships help address reliability and scaling requirements.

**Supply Chain & Optics Players**  
LITE, COHR, and AAOI continue to track AI infrastructure cycles, with component demand (lasers, engines, interconnects) supporting structural growth. Taiwanese specialists (FOCI/3363, ShunSin/6451) and SIVE benefit indirectly through increased testing, analysis, and packaging activity. Recent moves by analysis providers signal that upstream readiness for higher-volume CPO deployments in switches — and eventual GPU-scale integration — is progressing.

**Core CPO Takeaways**  
• CPO adoption path is methodical: early scale-out switch deployments in 2026, followed by scale-up optical I/O ramps later in the decade. Power savings, density gains, and reduced laser count remain the primary value drivers.  
• Supporting ecosystem (packaging, testing, lasers, analysis) is expanding in parallel, helping de-risk supply chain bottlenecks for hyperscalers.

CPO remains a multi-year structural theme in AI infrastructure. Watch for continued execution signals from platform leaders and incremental supply chain expansions as 2026 volume targets approach. Long-term visibility favors companies enabling the optics transition across the full stack.

#CPO #CoPackagedOptics #AIInfrastructure #Photonics #Semiconductors

text:
Media sparked  $SIVE panic by framing the photonics chief's sales as insider flight, completely missing his retirement transition.

The real signal? The CEO & board members immediately bought the dip on the open market.

Journalism created the discount. Insiders bought it.

Here is what I think the article can have missed. Andrew McKee founded CST now Sivers Photonics in 1999— 27 years ago. He built the photonics business from scratch. Earlier this year Alex McCann appeared publicly as the new MD of Sivers Photonics in April/May communications. McKee moved totechnology role only. His Q1 and Q2 share sales seems to align with this transition and was probably sold (if sold at all in full) parts of it even before share re-rating. My take is that is was done probably with no negative view on the company's prospects. A man monetizing his life's work after almost 30 years of building a company is not insider panic. IMO this is probably more a career ending on his own terms. He deserves it for sure. Even if he sold over 6 months, IMO this is still not a major negative signal. Three things EFN did not check: A) Holdings has known blind spots — transfers to holding companies, ISK structures and family vehicles are not captured. Did anyone verify McKee holds zero shares in any structure? B) One option program was realized in early July. If McKee sold old shares to participate that is not a major negative signal (he is not insider hence would not report sell or buy) — Holdings would not show this yet since it was in early July C) The role change probably happened before most shares was sold, Context matters. Later in the afternoon two board members has publicly shared insider purchases. Todd Thomson — board member, former Citigroup CFO, — bought 535,000 SEK on-market today at 42.80 SEK into the panic. Karin Raj bought 460,000 SEK at 34.68 SEK — the intraday low. Both reported to FI same day. People with more information about the full picture, that also had been part of approving today clarifications around the Q-reports and US Nasdaq listing. Update Vikram CEO have also aquired shares for approximately 1.000.000 SEK Summing up: Journalism created the dip. Insiders bought it. Daily Hard Tech Headlines:

- @ForterraDrive, Caterpillar Inc., IDV, and @Overland_AI_X have been selected for a new U.S. Army program focused on autonomous ground vehicles for battlefield breaching.

- The U.S. Army tested Autonomous Volcano, a @ForterraDrive-enabled driverless truck that can autonomously deploy minefields to block enemy movement.

- Kraken Technology Group and Capewell, with support from the Royal Navy, have successfully airdropped an uncrewed surface vessel from a military cargo aircraft for maritime missions.

- @reservoirfarms, the on-farm agtech and robotics incubator, is expanding into Arizona with access to 500 acres for specialty crop automation testing.

- Kraken Technology Group secured a $175M Series B led by DTCP. 

- @ForterraDrive autonomous ground vehicles have completed 1,100+ frontline missions in Ukraine, with Starlink supporting connectivity.

- Pulse Space has secured a $40M U.S. Space Force award to advance directed-energy infrastructure for space operations, using laser systems to track objects in orbit.

- @MistralAI, the French frontier AI lab, has launched a robotics model focused on warehouse and logistics automation.

- QX LOGISTIX, the Southern California logistics firm, plans to operate 20 Tesla Semi trucks for middle-mile logistics.

- Black Forest Systems has secured a €450,000 funding round. The European startup develops ground and aerial autonomy technology.

- @Leonid_CP, the critical-industry private credit firm, has deployed $6.5M+ to Rx Bandz, which develops compact auto-injectors for emergency and military medicine.

- Leidos has partnered with Rune Technologies to integrate contested logistics technology for predictive military logistics in the Indo-Pacific.

- @fervoenergy, the geothermal technology startup, completed its fastest Cape Station well yet, reaching 19,448 feet in 21 days as drilling rates improved 143% from its first well at the site.

- DOE is deploying up to $40M to support Thea Energy superconducting magnet technology for fusion power and Xscape Photonics Inc photonic computing technology for data centers.

- ispace, inc., the Japanese space company, is now offering SpaceX Starship capacity for lunar payload cargo.

- Talon Precision, Inc. has acquired B&A, an East Coast precision machine shop supporting the aerospace end market.

- @1x_tech, the Bay Area robotics firm, has launched new hands for its NEO humanoid robot, enabling 25 degrees of freedom through a tendon-driven design built for near human-level control.

gigawatts (GW) between 2026 and 2030. > Surging Demand vs. Capped Supply: Global semiconductor team forecasts imply a need for 230+ GW of capacity demand, while the US utilities team expects only 93 GW of accredited supply from regulated utilities. > Massive Compute Load: Driven by AI accelerators, global IT load is expected to require 208 GW between 2026 and 2030. Factoring in a 50% North American share and a 1.20 Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) multiplier to account for cooling and facilities, this translates to 125 GW of direct US data center load growth. > Accelerated Growth Rate: After flat growth from 2010 to 2020 (largely due to a 150bp drag from LED adoption, appliance efficiency, and residential solar), US electrical load is projected to grow at a 4.1% CAGR from 2026 to 2030. > Rise of Gas Engines: Due to turbine scarcity and the need for fast-response grid balancing, data center developers are shifting to gas reciprocating engines. Leading manufacturers like Caterpillar, INNIO Group, Rolls Royce, and Wärtsilä have all announced capacity expansions. > Exponential Rack-Level Power Growth: BofA Global Research pointed out a staggering leap in the power density required by Nvidia's hardware generations. Power density has jumped from 35 kilowatts (kW) per rack for the Nvidia H100 chip up to an estimated 600 kW per rack for its upcoming Feynman architecture. $GEV $CAT $NVDA $AMD $GOOGL $AMZN $INTC"}}}, "rest_id": "2075064973990998315", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "22648", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2075319497204875658", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "144", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Shit show? 

Why do you write this kind of things? You seem to be a knowledge person? I built Sivers from $2m to +$20m for almost 10 years. I was part of putting in on Nasdaq Stockholm with one of the most critical auditors doing the review, if you worked at EY you know how challenging that is. If you actually know Photonics, as it says in your profile, you should know Sivers, Ayar labs, GF, Coherent, Lumentum, MACOM etc created the CW laser stands before the AI boom. The exact technology now needed for AI.  Ayar labs one off the most respected AI photonics companies chose Sivers lasers before Lumentum and MACOM. Please do your research and be honest. I am not talking about vaule that is up to the market to decide, just how you write about the company.

text:
Yeah, SemiAnalysis dropped a bearish photonics note on June 9, and about 3 weeks later they co-launched an ETF that holds $LITE. Hard to say it’s not intentional.

This is why I’d rather build conviction from my own work than trust a note that an analysis company posted. They act in their own interests.

text:
UDN Money report "殺翻光通訊後 SemiAnalysis 惹議 業界揭其劣跡 許多公司慘遭魔手":

- SemiAnalysis, released a report in June, questioning the timing of the launch of the CPO on the grounds of low yield and delay, which caused the photonics sector to collapse. 

- The agency then partnered with Tema to launch a photonics ETF. 

- The shares held in the ETF under the trading code LAZR happened to be the photonics stocks that were mentioned negatively in the report

$HIMX and $LITE, which were specifically targeted negatively in their article "Powered Down, Lights Off", have been bought in Semianalysis and Tema's new ETF after the optical sector crash.

text:
blog's up 👇

short version - chips stopped shrinking, so the money moved from making silicon to packaging it. in silicon photonics, packaging is ~60-70% of the chip.

india spent $18bn on semis and spent almost all of it in commodity OSAT - the low margin part. the high-value, advanced packaging + photonics - has no listed indian pure-play in India.

except one - a small auto-software company from bangalore claiming india's only photonics packaging line - the tech story is genuinely interesting considering how they have build the capablity but the governance file is kinda scary.

not a reco. just us following the value chain.

https://t.co/PTZxygiWPR

we've been reading a lot about advanced packaging (thanks to citrini year's start blog) and how we can play it via Indian players. 

we are pretty much stuck at OSAT on ground level given we have been a little* behind in terms of getting those capablities (a little😅)

text:
#IZMO 
Silicon Photonics (SiPh) represents a massive fundamental shift in how data moves, driven by the structural bottlenecks of artificial intelligence workloads.

1. The Silicon Photonics Market: Why the Indirection Matters
Historically, microchips communicated internally and externally using electrons traveling over copper wires. However, as high-performance GenAI training clusters, large language models (LLMs), and hyperscale data centers expand, copper has hit a physical brick wall known as RC delay and skin effect. At extreme speeds, copper wires generate unsustainable heat, consume excessive power, and suffer severe signal degradation.

Silicon Photonics replaces electrons with photons (light). By fabricating optical waveguides directly onto silicon substrates, chips can transmit data using lasers.

Main Market Verticals (2026 Trajectory)
AI Clusters & Datacom: The primary growth engine. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) fuse optical engines right next to the compute ASIC/GPU, expanding transceiver bandwidth past 1.6T and 3.2T.

Aerospace & Defense: High-frequency Electronic Warfare (EW), phased-array radar signal processing, and space payloads where weight reduction and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) are critical.

Quantum Computing: Distributing entangled photons across processing nodes via low-loss optical interconnects.

Major Indian Competitors 
India’s domestic chip landscape is heavily skewed toward legacy assembly, testing, and marking (ATMP) or standard wire-bonded packaging. True Silicon Photonics capability is highly restricted

IZMO’s Specific Role in the Value Chain: The Packaging Moat
In Silicon Photonics, packaging is the ultimate commercial bottleneck, routinely accounting for 60% to 80% of the total component cost.

While aligning electrical microbumps allows for minor margins of error, aligning an optical fiber to a silicon chip's internal sub-micron waveguide requires nanometer-scale precision. A misalignment of even a few hundred nanometers can cause catastrophic insertion loss, rendering the optical link useless.

Through its subsidiary, izmomicro (based in Whitefield, Bengaluru), Izmo has deliberately chosen not to compete in the high-cap-ex, low-margin foundry or standard assembly market. Instead, it has positioned itself as a specialized, high-barrier Advanced Packaging Partner.

The Silicon Photonics Market: Why the Indirection Matters Historically, microchips communicated internally and externally using electrons traveling over copper wires. However, as high-performance GenAI training clusters, large language models (LLMs), and hyperscale data centers expand, copper has hit a physical brick wall known as RC delay and skin effect. At extreme speeds, copper wires generate unsustainable heat, consume excessive power, and suffer severe signal degradation. Silicon Photonics replaces electrons with photons (light). By fabricating optical waveguides directly onto silicon substrates, chips can transmit data using lasers. Main Market Verticals (2026 Trajectory) AI Clusters & Datacom: The primary growth engine. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) fuse optical engines right next to the compute ASIC/GPU, expanding transceiver bandwidth past 1.6T and 3.2T. Aerospace & Defense: High-frequency Electronic Warfare (EW), phased-array radar signal processing, and space payloads where weight reduction and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) are critical. Quantum Computing: Distributing entangled photons across processing nodes via low-loss optical interconnects. Major Indian Competitors India’s domestic chip landscape is heavily skewed toward legacy assembly, testing, and marking (ATMP) or standard wire-bonded packaging. True Silicon Photonics capability is highly restricted IZMO’s Specific Role in the Value Chain: The Packaging Moat In Silicon Photonics, packaging is the ultimate commercial bottleneck, routinely accounting for 60% to 80% of the total component cost. While aligning electrical microbumps allows for minor margins of error, aligning an optical fiber to a silicon chip's internal sub-micron waveguide requires nanometer-scale precision. A misalignment of even a few hundred nanometers can cause catastrophic insertion loss, rendering the optical link useless. Through its subsidiary, izmomicro (based in Whitefield, Bengaluru), Izmo has deliberately chosen not to compete in the high-cap-ex, low-margin foundry or standard assembly market. Instead, it has positioned itself as a specialized, high-barrier Advanced Packaging Partner."}}}, "rest_id": "2073443344861282413", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "484", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Paperhand shorts means that the short covering could be aggressive if/when it happens

Regardless, don't think it's smart relying on short covering but the photonics sector without any shorts covering also looks very attractive here after large drawdowns across the board 

There are a lot of companies in this theme/sector to research, but for the sake of simplicity it might be best to just buy a photonics ETF since we can't predict which individual names perform well or not

Or, for people who prefer individual stocks, a simple pick for quality would be $LITE

Or a more risky, volatile and perhaps more upside if it plays out: $AAOI

If you are worried about CPO delays, a good way to benefit from photonics and copper is also to be long $CRDO

Optical stocks have traded down for 1-2 months on two stories: fears that Chinese indium phosphide capacity crashes laser pricing for $LITE $COHR $AAOI, and reports that scale up CPO slips a year. Rosenblatt figures Chinese players add 20-40% to their 2030 InP forecast, but they already model 2030 capacity running 40% behind demand. So even with new supply the industry is still short lasers. They also argue the CPO "delay" is overblown. The process isn't delayed, only the ramp to volume. 2028 is now the inflection year and NPO fills the 2027 gap with nearly identical laser needs. Same photons, same suppliers, same thesis. You might see photonics red right now but the smart money is setting up to reload here ahead of earnings. $AAOI has been hit particularly hard and it's well known Rosenblatt has the highest street PT. So it's nice to see a bullish note here ahead of ER. "We expect AAOI to beat and raise and for 2Q26 to mark a watershed quarter where this starts happening consistently. We believe 2Q26 revenues can beat, and be above $200mn, with 800G sales to Amazon and Oracle as the primary driver of upside. drop 🔖 I've never come across anyone on FinX talking about this gem: $NOVT. So… I guess I'll be the one to talk about it. It's one of my favorite companies to own for the long term right now, as they provide a foundational hardware layer for the physical AI / advanced robotics revolution that's about to unfold. ⌁ Momentum $NOVT has crossed into sustained demand acceleration powered by technologies that enable physical intelligence. Every single business unit delivered double-digit bookings growth this quarter. Bookings overall jumped 25% YoY, and the company posted its first positive book-to-bill across the board since 2022; it came in at 1.11. That's real evidence that design wins are converting. ⌁ Physical AI Management highlighted their work in intelligent physical AI solutions for care, warehouse automation, humanoids, and precision robotics. Their servo drives, force-torque sensors, and motion subsystems give robots the ability to perceive and react safely in the real world. CFO Buckley: "We feel it is really superior to anything out there from a competitive perspective." They're involved in the ISO standards that will govern this space and are already working with pretty much everybody in humanoids and leading warehouse players. Buckley: "And you can see we are working with pretty much everybody out there when it comes to the humanoid markets and the leading players in warehouse automation. So we feel really good about that technology. [...] But we could not be better positioned, both industry-wise and customer-wise in hoping to grapple on to that opportunity." The management is clearly telling you that the company is a major physical AI pick and shovel play. The long term potential is MASSIVE. New product sales grew over 80% in the quarter. The vitality index (new product revenues as a percent of total revenues) hit 24% of revenue. These are the metrics that matter when content multiplies across joints and end-effectors as humanoids scale. ⌁ GPU drilling moat One line stood out to me. The CEO (Glastra) explicitly said that their air bearing spindles are the only qualified supplier for drilling the complex boards that power next-generation GPUs. This business is growing at strong double-digit rates with a multi-year trajectory ahead. Most people focus on the silicon. Few think about the mechanical precision layer that actually enables the build-out. $NOVT is directly plugged into the AI infrastructure capex cycle. Wall Street has completely failed to model this vector imo. ⌁ Medical precision engine On the medical side, advanced surgery grew mid-teens last year and should deliver double digits again in 2026. The next-gen insufflators are setting safety and workflow standards, and they're expanding even more into robotic surgery and arthroscopy. Consumables have reached 15% of company revenue with strong growth. Long term they see this becoming a roughly $400 million platform. ⌁ Last thoughts What I find particularly compelling is the multi-vector exposure: Embodiment for humanoids and warehouse robots. Critical infrastructure for AI compute scaling. Laser systems for advanced manufacturing. A growing recurring revenue base in surgery robotics. $NOVT enables AI to interact with the physical world. For those of us tracking deep tech at the intersection of AI and real-world systems, this setup deserves close attention."}}}, "rest_id": "2027838306239844529", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "416", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2075664946931835390", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "243", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Silex is a swedish player that positions itself as the largest pure-play MEMS foundry, with 200mm production capabilities and expertise in complex designs, with the potential to capture opportunities in sensor monitoring, photonics, cloud infrastructure, medical applications, and autonomous vehicles.

It's not a direct competitor of $SITM on MEMS but a manufacturing competitor in the SiTime supply chain.

text:
AI's Next Bottleneck: Networking & Optics Could Become the Biggest Winners of the AI Infrastructure Cycle
The AI investment narrative has largely centered on GPUs and compute. However, the next phase of the AI supercycle may be determined by an entirely different segment: networking and optical infrastructure.
As AI clusters expand from thousands to millions of accelerators, moving data efficiently becomes just as important as generating compute power. Faster GPUs create little value if they spend time waiting for information to arrive.
That is why the Networking & Optics Landscape deserves close attention.
Current industry data highlights an unprecedented supply-demand imbalance:
Coherent's Indium Phosphide (InP) lasers are reportedly sold out through 2027, supported by an exceptional 4x book-to-bill ratio.
Ciena reached a record $7B order backlog, reflecting sustained demand from hyperscalers and telecom infrastructure.
Lumentum expects approximately 77% revenue growth for FY2026, one of the strongest growth outlooks across semiconductor-adjacent industries.
Unlike many AI segments where additional manufacturing capacity is gradually coming online, optical networking remains a genuine bottleneck. Every new AI data center requires high-speed optical interconnects, silicon photonics, switches, and advanced networking hardware before GPUs can operate at scale.
The companies highlighted in this landscape represent nearly every critical layer of the ecosystem:
Switching & Systems: Cisco, Arista, Ciena
Interconnect Silicon: Marvell, Astera Labs, Credo, Macom
Lasers & Optical Components: Coherent, Lumentum, Corning, Applied Optoelectronics
Manufacturing: Fabrinet
China Optics: Innolight, Eoptolink
Silicon Photonics (Private): Lightmatter, Ayar Labs
Crypto Investors Should Pay Attention
Blockchain investors often focus on tokens directly tied to AI narratives, but the real capital cycle frequently begins with physical infrastructure.
As hyperscalers continue deploying massive AI clusters, companies supplying networking hardware and optical components may capture significant value before downstream AI applications fully mature.
The lesson for crypto markets is familiar: infrastructure tends to outperform during the early stages of transformational technology cycles. The same pattern previously benefited semiconductor manufacturers, cloud providers, and blockchain infrastructure projects.
The AI race is no longer just about faster chips—it's increasingly about moving data at unprecedented speed. Until supply catches demand, networking and optics remain one of the strongest structural themes across the entire AI ecosystem.

However, the next phase of the AI supercycle may be determined by an entirely different segment: networking and optical infrastructure. As AI clusters expand from thousands to millions of accelerators, moving data efficiently becomes just as important as generating compute power. Faster GPUs create little value if they spend time waiting for information to arrive. That is why the Networking & Optics Landscape deserves close attention. Current industry data highlights an unprecedented supply-demand imbalance: Coherent's Indium Phosphide (InP) lasers are reportedly sold out through 2027, supported by an exceptional 4x book-to-bill ratio. Ciena reached a record $7B order backlog, reflecting sustained demand from hyperscalers and telecom infrastructure. Lumentum expects approximately 77% revenue growth for FY2026, one of the strongest growth outlooks across semiconductor-adjacent industries. Unlike many AI segments where additional manufacturing capacity is gradually coming online, optical networking remains a genuine bottleneck. Every new AI data center requires high-speed optical interconnects, silicon photonics, switches, and advanced networking hardware before GPUs can operate at scale. The companies highlighted in this landscape represent nearly every critical layer of the ecosystem: Switching & Systems: Cisco, Arista, Ciena Interconnect Silicon: Marvell, Astera Labs, Credo, Macom Lasers & Optical Components: Coherent, Lumentum, Corning, Applied Optoelectronics Manufacturing: Fabrinet China Optics: Innolight, Eoptolink Silicon Photonics (Private): Lightmatter, Ayar Labs Crypto Investors Should Pay Attention Blockchain investors often focus on tokens directly tied to AI narratives, but the real capital cycle frequently begins with physical infrastructure. As hyperscalers continue deploying massive AI clusters, companies supplying networking hardware and optical components may capture significant value before downstream AI applications fully mature. The lesson for crypto markets is familiar: infrastructure tends to outperform during the early stages of transformational technology cycles. The same pattern previously benefited semiconductor manufacturers, cloud providers, and blockchain infrastructure projects. The AI race is no longer just about faster chips—it's increasingly about moving data at unprecedented speed. Until supply catches demand, networking and optics remain one of the strongest structural themes across the entire AI ecosystem. #AI #Crypto #Stocks #Tech"}}}, "rest_id": "2075654452158300439", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "74", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Agentic AI is starting to do the work of a full scouting team, now in private markets. @earlysignalsvc  points it at private tech companies and scores them the week they raise, launch, or leave stealth, not the week they trend.

The whole edge in markets is acting while a signal is still fresh, before everyone else has read the same headline. Public equities are already a crowded place to try that, which is the game the DeepSeek Portfolio plays every week. Doing it in venture, at the moment a company raises or leaves stealth, is a much earlier point on the curve.

Forty-four companies ranked this week, each on six scored dimensions plus a potential and a scoop score. Photonics for AI data centers, infrastructure for long-running agents, on-device writing tools. Systematic scoring pushed all the way to the front of the funnel.

Good work. Worth a look.

text:
This post from relatively unknown account @0xBADB01E is going viral within the AI community - here's why

The core claim is genuinely contrarian and flips how we think about scaling architectures and supply chain constraints: 

> premise: the wires between chips matter more than the chips themselves or the memory attached to them

> if you designed an AI inference machine starting from the interconnect and working backwards, you'd build something radically different (and cheaper) than an Nvidia GPU

> GPUs are terrible at generating tokens - when an LLM generates text one token at a time (decode), a Blackwell GPU uses under 20% of its math capability as the compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from memory

IOW, you bought a Ferrari engine and it spends 80% of its time waiting at the gas pump

> the intuitive answer is "add faster memory" (more HBM) but the smarter answer is: since a single chip can't have infinite memory bandwidth, split the model across many chips and let each chip handle a slice

> the problem then shifts because the constraint is no longer memory bandwidth, it's how fast chips can talk to each other (the interconnect)

> Nvidia's interconnect is the wrong approach - NVLink is a firehose with huge bandwidth, but high latency (delay per message)

> that was a deliberate choice for training, but not ideal for inference where the data crossing between chips per token is absurdly tiny 

It's like paying a $50 shipping-and-handling fee on a $1 product

The contrarian take is that everyone says "decode is memory-bandwidth bound," but once you shard the model, per-user speed is gated by how quickly the tiny inter-chip messages complete, not by how fat the memory pipe is - decode is latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound

> if latency is what matters, you can build around slow, cheap memory (LPDDR instead of HBM), because its access latency is about the same as HBM's; only its bandwidth is worse

> then you only need enough FLOPs to match that memory

The above two statements have big implications:

1. this is a direct thesis against HBM's centrality in the compute buildout

2. the packaging supply chain that's currently the binding constraint on Nvidia's output matters much less

3. for photonics, it can potentially go either way - depends on if optical is focused on improving bandwidth (bearish) or latency (bullish)

I don't know this account but this post was interesting and intelligent so I'm here for the conversation that follows

"media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "This post from relatively unknown account @0xBADB01E is going viral within the AI community - here's why The core claim is genuinely contrarian and flips how we think about scaling architectures and supply chain constraints: > premise: the wires between chips matter more than the chips themselves or the memory attached to them > if you designed an AI inference machine starting from the interconnect and working backwards, you'd build something radically different (and cheaper) than an Nvidia GPU > GPUs are terrible at generating tokens - when an LLM generates text one token at a time (decode), a Blackwell GPU uses under 20% of its math capability as the compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from memory IOW, you bought a Ferrari engine and it spends 80% of its time waiting at the gas pump > the intuitive answer is "add faster memory" (more HBM) but the smarter answer is: since a single chip can't have infinite memory bandwidth, split the model across many chips and let each chip handle a slice > the problem then shifts because the constraint is no longer memory bandwidth, it's how fast chips can talk to each other (the interconnect) > Nvidia's interconnect is the wrong approach - NVLink is a firehose with huge bandwidth, but high latency (delay per message) > that was a deliberate choice for training, but not ideal for inference where the data crossing between chips per token is absurdly tiny It's like paying a $50 shipping-and-handling fee on a $1 product The contrarian take is that everyone says "decode is memory-bandwidth bound," but once you shard the model, per-user speed is gated by how quickly the tiny inter-chip messages complete, not by how fat the memory pipe is - decode is latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound > if latency is what matters, you can build around slow, cheap memory (LPDDR instead of HBM), because its access latency is about the same as HBM's; only its bandwidth is worse > then you only need enough FLOPs to match that memory The above two statements have big implications: 1. this is a direct thesis against HBM's centrality in the compute buildout 2. the packaging supply chain that's currently the binding constraint on Nvidia's output matters much less 3. for photonics, it can potentially go either way - depends on if optical is focused on improving bandwidth (bearish) or latency (bullish) I don't know this account but this post was interesting and intelligent so I'm here for the conversation that follows"}}}, As an example,", "id_str": "2073990357398982797", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "0xBADB01E", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "2073970411138949533", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "1811787044722479105", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 55, "reply_count": 138, "retweet_count": 192, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1811787044722479105"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzM5OTAzNTcxNzI1NTE2ODA=", "text": "First you have to understand that modern LLM inference already disaggregates weights as models outgrew single chips years ago. You shard either by layer (pipeline parallelism) or by slicing every layer (tensor parallelism), and the two do very different things. As an example, let’s look at Llama 3.3. It has 70B of weights and at FP8 that’s 70 GB of memory which is enough to fit on a single H100. Now that H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM, so the fastest it can ever decode for one user is 70/3.35 ≈ 21 ms/token or ~48 tok/s while using under 1% of its FLOPs. Now if we pipeline it across 8 chips: each chip holds ~8.75 GB, which means it only needs 1/8th the bandwidth and 1/8th the FLOPs to sustain the same aggregate throughput. Now crucially the token/sec a user gets is limited by the amount of data that crosses the link. In current LLMs all that is a small amount of activations for LLama 3.3 it’s ~8 KB per token…. Yes, you read that right it’s 8 KILOBYTES we are sending over a <900 GB/s link. That’s only 9 ns of serialization time but the overhead of 224G PAM4 SerDes adds ~100 ns per link traversal with RS-FEC which is 11x longer than the payload itself. And then you have the NVSwitch adding ~300 ns per hop and you need to pay twice. That’s ~600 ns of just hardware latency wrapped around 9 ns of data making a 98% tax before software even shows up. Then NCCL’s collective stack turns 600 ns into 10-20+ us… all to move 8 kilobytes lol. For comparison 8 KB serializes over 10 Gigabit Ethernet NRZ, in just 6.6 us. Pipeline parallelism however doesn’t make a single user faster as the token still needs to visit every layer in the sequence, so per-user speed is still weights / per-chip bandwidth. To get more speed per user token you need to use tensor parallelism and have all the chips work on the same layer simultaneously. TP costs you 2 all reduce OPs per layer, 160 per token on llama 3, that’s still kilobytes of traffic but with NVLink overhead it’s a massive tax and why pipeline parallelism on most models still gives more interactivity per user. However, this gives you a huge latency lever to pull that scales tokens per second with interconnect speed instead of memory BW. The clever amongst you might have also realized that sharding doesn’t just cut memory bandwidth per chip it also cuts FLOPs per chip and is why we have such bad MFU on decode. So once you’ve sized the link for the memory, you need to size the compute for it too. This is called “balancing the pipeline”, and currently no shipping chip does it because they were all designed as standalone monsters. Remember Tokens/sec = ~aggregate memory BW / bytes touched per token. At batch 64 in FP4 you need ~250 FLOPs per byte, and Blackwell ships 1,250. Provisioned 5x more than the narrow pipe of HBM. Nobody saturates shit cause they are all building around HBM. So now it all comes full circle. Parallelism reduces memory bw pressure and thus FLOPs but increases interconnect latency pressure. Despite having HBM and GigaSERDES we aren’t actually doing more work lol. But if you really wanted to balance the pipeline you need to match the memory bandwidth, the flops, and most importantly the interconnect. So what does that look like ? Well if you build around LPDDR’s lower bandwidth, lower your interconnect latency, you actually can beat Nvidia on decode with a fraction of the silicon."}}}, "rest_id": "2073990357398982797", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1490889", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074201112006242337", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5066", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$BURU @NUBURUlasers is extending its laser platform from counter-UAS to underwater autonomy.

Through Lyocon Italian laser-tech factory and photonics center of excellence, NUBURU is working with Suncubes to connect blue-laser technology, beam-control and optical power transmission into new Defense & Security applications.

✅Recent PRs highlight two strategic directions:
- Laser Arm: vehicle-integrated directed-energy and counter-UAS systems.
- DEEP LIGHT: blue-laser power-beaming for underwater autonomy, subsea power and maritime infrastructure.

The opportunity is not only one project. It is the potential validation of NUBURU blue-laser technology in mission-critical environments where power, endurance and optical performance matter.
Lyocon provides the laser engine. 
☀️SunCubes provides beam-control and optical power capabilities. NUBURU builds the platform.

Subject to definitive agreements, technical validation, regulatory clearances and applicable approvals.
#NUBURU #Lyocon #SunCubes #BlueLaser  #DefenseTech #DirectedEnergy  #CounterUAS  #UnderwaterAutonomy

text:
amazing!

"MIT researchers developed 2 new photonic coupling technologies, the Evanescent Coupler and GRIN Coupler, that could enable future chip packages to exceed 1 petabit per second (1,000 Tb/s) of communication bandwidth."

"Instead of sending data through power hungry copper wires, the system uses light (photonics) to move information between chips, dramatically increasing bandwidth while reducing energy consumption and heat."

"Researchers designed the technology to be compatible with existing CMOS semiconductor manufacturing, potentially making ultra-high bandwidth photonic chip packages practical for future AI accelerators, supercomputers and hyperscale data centers."

text:
There's a company almost no one is talking about, trading near NAV, that sells into three of the hottest markets right now: AI chipmaking, defense batteries, and photonics.

And it has several unfair advantages:
→ $100M Department of Energy grant.
→ Samsung SDI building and backing its battery factory.
→ A 2028 law that bans the US military from buying Chinese battery cells.

That company is @ForgeNano, and the vehicle taking it public at a $1.2B valuation is Archimedes Tech SPAC Partners II $ATII, run by the same team that took SoundHound $SOUN public in 2022.

Forge Nano sells atomic-scale coatings, protective layers thousands of times thinner than a human hair, that make batteries last longer and chips fail less.

$ATII trades at $10.88/share today against a trust worth ~$10.63/share, so the downside from here is ~2%. And you can buy $ATIIW if you want the leveraged upside via warrants.

I've done deep dives (and bought) into SPAC setups like this before:
→ Boost Run $BRUN pre-merger (originally $WLAC).
→ Exascale Labs $BCAR, still pre-merger.

My full 6k-word deep dive on $ATII will be released tomorrow. Link in bio to read it when it comes out!

Thanks to @SPAC_zilla and @stepnotonpets for the idea!

text:
Market Review (Mid-Day) 20260707:
- Semi and Memory washout this morning, BUT still trying to hang in there. Basically all the prior hardware leading sectors, including Photonics, Data Centres etc.
- I've been banging the drum about money rotating OUT of AI HARDWARE for about 2 weeks now. So this is no surprise. The AI Trade is NOT over. I just see money rotating now to the next layer. 
- Where? Remember I talked about Cybersecurity and selected software? It is becoming clearer that it is mostly storage and database management related. That is why DELL, HPE, NTAP, SNOW, DDOG, RBRK, all continue to do well. These guys STORE and MANAGE the Data. And then we have CRWD PANW FTNT. These guys PROTECT the data. RDDT seems to be ok too. They have a lot of data. 
- See what is the common denominator? DATA. At the end of the day, AI needs data to function to its best ability. And that data needs to be stored and managed properly. 
- There is another layer on top of the Data layer, which is usage / application. Biotech is a clear beneficiary and that's why its been leading recently. Fintech seems to take to it easily as well, and has been doing well too. 
- Interestingly, one of my favourite companies at the moment, ZETA, play both in the DATA and benefits from USAGE. But it’s a very volatile small cap. Not for everyone. 
- So, what now?
- I don’t know your investment horizon. If long enough, the AI hardware trade I think will come back at some point. It needs to base and rest and for now. Just beware that often, distribution / topping sometimes looks like basing / resting. We only know after the fact. So we have to bear that in mind as a possible scenario. What I am saying is that, using Memory as an example, it is POSSIBLE that it has topped. I do not know and don't want to call it, but I have it at the back of my mind as a POSSIBILITY. MU, 10X in a year. What more do you want? Yeah, I know it has low PE, forward orders blah blah blah. They all look like that at tops. The most important question is, WHO ELSE is out there who needs to buy that hasn’t already bought? 
- So, instead of continuing to buy / hold something that has POTENTIALLY topped, why not buy something else which has a higher probability of continuing the uptrend? Which is why the portfolio started to shift about 2-3 weeks ago into these other stocks. You will notice it if you have been copying my port for a while. In Apr and May, we were full of semiconductors, memory, photonics etc. Software in late May. And since then mainly the themes I talked about above. 
- Other themes that are kindda standing out are consumer staples, quality stocks, financials, retail. This is why I am not totally bearish on the market overall. Market is just rotating to other sectors at the moment, NOT leaving the market totally. I don't see outright panic selling (though you might feel differently if you are holding all the previously hot stocks like AMD, INTC, AMAT, SNDK, MU, WDC, ALAB, MRVL, AAOI, LITE). 
- One small tell is worth mentioning. WULF announced a major deal with Anthropic yesterday and gapped up at the open by about 17%? It sold off through the day, and today took out the deal announcement low as well as the prior day low! News and REACTION like this is very important for me. Remember I talked about how WDC could not hold the good news from MU? That was one canary in the coal mine. Then MU could not hold. Then Samsung. And WULF. ALL the hardware names simply could not catch a bid from good news. And if stocks cannot go up on good news, what do you expect them to do? There's only one other direction from there…
- So, for me, I am avoiding most of the AI hardware players for now. And focusing on the DATABASE and SECURITY related players going forward. 
- Just my 2 cents. Could be wrong. DYOR. NFA.

Chip-Scale Ultrafast Lasers Could Bring Femtosecond Performance to Portable Photonics

https://t.co/vl1knOZqWI 

#ChipScale #UltraFastLasers #Femtosecond #PortablePhotonics #RPMCLasers https://t.co/6vgXBgACDI

A Small Business Today, But Management's Ambition Is Much Bigger

At the moment, IzmoMicro is still a relatively small business. It generated around ₹18-19 crore of revenue in FY26, but management expects this to increase to ₹45-50 crore in FY27.

More importantly, the building blocks are gradually falling into place. The company already has an order book of around ₹40 crore, a pipeline of nearly ₹100 crore, partnerships with IIT Madras, Alcyon Photonics and CCRAFT, and has established a presence in Germany to access European semiconductor customers. 

Management's long-term aspiration is to build IzmoMicro into a ₹1,200-1,500 crore business over the next five years.

text:
So Where Does IZMO Fit In?

This is where many investors misunderstand the story.
IZMO is not trying to manufacture AI chips. Instead, it is building one of the most critical parts that allows those chips to work efficiently - advanced semiconductor packaging and silicon photonics packaging. 

In simple words, even the most powerful chip is of little use if it cannot communicate quickly with the rest of the system.

What's interesting is that as semiconductor technology becomes more complex, packaging itself is becoming increasingly valuable. Management shared that while traditional packaging contributes only 8-10% of a chip's value, advanced packaging contributes around 50%, and silicon photonics packaging can account for almost 80%. That is a remarkable shift in where value is being created.

text:
The Biggest AI Opportunity Isn't Always the Chip

Whenever AI is discussed, companies like NVIDIA usually dominate the conversation. Everyone wants faster GPUs, more computing power and larger AI models.

But there's another problem quietly becoming just as important - moving data between those chips. As AI models become larger, the amount of information flowing inside data centres is exploding. 

Traditional copper connections are slowly reaching their limits, and that's where the industry is turning towards silicon photonics, which uses light instead of electricity to move data much faster.

IZMO: The ₹50 Crore Business That Could Become a ₹1,500 Crore Opportunity

IZMO is no longer just an automotive software company. It is quietly building a semiconductor packaging business that management believes can grow from ~₹50 crore in FY27 to ₹1,200-1,500 crore over the next five years.

Here's everything you need to know about IzmoMicro, silicon photonics, advanced packaging, and why management believes this could become the company's biggest growth engine. 🧵👇

text: Photonic qubits encode quantum data in particles of light. A key advantage: they can operate at room temp, unlike systems needing extreme cold. This makes them ideal for networking, but creating gates is tough because photons don't easily interact. #QuantumComputing #Photonics https://t.co/ErlDYIQIzb

text:
The Bottleneck In AI Just Met Its Match

As AI continues to scale, the conversation is shifting beyond compute to the infrastructure that powers it.

We're proud to see Neural I/o™ featured in @TheRundownAI, highlighting how Kopin and FabricAI are advancing MicroLED-based optical interconnect technology designed to address the bandwidth, latency, and power challenges of next-generation AI infrastructure.

Explore the feature to learn more and register to access our upcoming Neural I/o™ White Paper, where we take a deeper dive into the future of AI connectivity.

Learn more & register: https://t.co/V7yGqbR3hx

#KopinTech #ArtificialIntelligence #AIInfrastructure #MicroLED #Photonics #NeuralIO @FabricAIFABC @MiTknlg

text:
That's right, I don't even know how many posts I've made by now, but my repost to Serenity absolutely had to be there

Totally agree

As I've explained 30 times already, a sale by a former Head of the Photonics branch at $SIVE

Which probably happened months ago, and considering he hasn't even held that position for months, could be due to a thousand reasons

The most likely one being his retirement after 27 years at the company

On the other hand, the purchase by 3 insiders, highlighted by the CEO's 1M SEK buy, is a clear demonstration of confidence

Especially when there were so many doubts regarding the postponement of the earnings report until after the lock-up period on the 16th

Where brainless bears were claiming it was done so they could dump all their shares before $SIVE goes bankrupt

Nothing could be further from the truth

Another thing I want to add is regarding people saying 1M SEK isn't that much for a CEO

That's a lie. You have to understand that $SIVE is a Swedish company, not an American one

I know that in America, CEOs are mega-millionaires making $200k–$500k a year

But in Europe, currency is worth more and prices are lower, so they make much less

So yes, 1M SEK is a lot; it represents several annual salaries for the CEO

And besides, the amount is the least of it, what matters about this buy is what it confirms

It confirms that they believe in the company and its potential to become a key player in the AI supply chain

So yes, this is incredibly good news for $SIVE

Long on $SIVE

text:
I spent some time reading $AUR latest engineering blog, and one thing really stood out to me.

They’ve built a custom silicon photonic engine for their FirstLight lidar, consolidating light generation, amplification, and detection into a compact multi-chip module.

I don’t think enough people appreciate how important this hardware moat is.
Getting autonomous driving software to work is only half the battle. You have to build hardware that is repeatable, reliable, and cost-effective enough to deploy at scale.

A few key takeaways:
• Fiber-free architecture: They eliminated fragile fiber connectors entirely, routing light through integrated waveguides to survive heavy-duty truck vibrations over hundreds of thousands of miles.

• Automated sub-micron assembly: Instead of slow, manual lab calibration, they use automated active alignment to position microlenses between chips in real-time on the production line.

• Vertical stack ownership: They design everything in-house, down to the epitaxial layers of their lasers, giving them total control over the automotive qualification process (PPAP) and allowing rapid architectural iteration.

As an investor, this is the stuff I pay attention to.

The AI gets all the headlines, but deep hardware engineering decisions like these determine whether a company can actually scale from hundreds of vehicles to tens of thousands.

This is another reason I’m bullish on $AUR and it continues to be my highest conviction moonshot play for the next decade. 

-T.E.E.

text:
WOOOOOOOW

So on the exact same day they try to sink us to death over an alleged sale by the "Head of Photonics $SIVE"

Not only do we recover and turn green

But insiders and the CEO himself bought the dip

All the stupid bears say they are selling

Meanwhile, the numbers show they are doing nothing but buying non stop

I warned you this was the bottom and you dared to laugh at me hahahahah pathetic bears

And now comes the funniest part of all:

When the millionaire positions of all those institutions that were spreading FUD about $SIVE finally come to light

If you still think $SIVE is going to 4 SEK

Instead of realizing that the fundamentals are intact and it was just pure manipulation for institutions to buy lower

Let me tell you, you are not very smart

Long on $SIVE

text:
Creating more energy?
Binary is on or off which takes power to turn on or off.
The most power is consumed when the bit changes state (e.g., flipping from a 0 to a 1).

Is there a lower power way to change state? Like colors or a state change in a lower temp environment?

AI:
Yes, there are much lower-power ways to change a bit's state, and your intuition about using colors and lower temperature environments is spot on with cutting-edge physics and computer engineering.
Modern computers waste a massive amount of energy pushing whole streams of electrons through wires. To bypass this, researchers are developing alternatives based exactly on the concepts you mentioned:
1. Lower Temperature Environments (The Physics Proof)
In physics, there is an absolute fundamental limit to how cheap a state change can be, called the Landauer's principle.
Why temperature matters: Notice the T(temperature) in the equation. Because temperature is directly multiplied by the energy cost, dropping the temperature drastically lowers the minimum energy required to flip a bit. In ultra-cold quantum computing or superconducting environments, the thermal "noise" is so low that bits can change state with a fraction of the energy required at room temperature.

2. Using Colors and Light (Photonics)
Instead of forcing electrons through a silicon transistor, scientists use Optical and Photonic Computing, which relies on light (colors/wavelengths).
Optical Switches: Instead of charging a capacitor with electricity, a pulse of light switches a material between being transparent or reflective.
The Power Advantage: Photons (light particles) do not have mass or electrical charge, meaning they do not generate heat through electrical resistance. Using different wavelengths (colors) allows data states to change at the speed of light with almost zero energy loss. Recent breakthroughs include devices combining magnetism with light to switch states in picoseconds using orders of magnitude less power than traditional electronics.
3. Structural State Changes (Phase-Change & Magnetics)
Beyond light and cold, there are structural ways to rewrite data using minimal energy:
Spintronics (Magnetic Bits): Instead of moving electrons from point A to point B, you simply change the spin (magnetic direction) of an electron right where it sits. This uses almost no moving current and has no capacitive electrical loss.
Phase-Change Memory (PCM): This approach mimics your idea of a physical state change. A tiny electrical pulse switches a glass-like material between an amorphous state (disorganized atoms = 0) and a crystalline state (organized atoms = 1). Once changed, it stays in that state forever without requiring any holding power.

text:
💰 Optical Chip Startup Ayar Labs Secures $500 Million at $3.75 Billion Valuation

1. Ayar Labs has successfully raised $500 million in a new funding round that values the semiconductor startup at $3.75 billion.
2. The company specializes in optical computing technology that uses light rather than electricity to move data between artificial intelligence chips.
3. Investors are increasingly backing alternative hardware architectures to diversify the supply chain and overcome the bandwidth bottlenecks in traditional silicon.

Bobbi thinks:
With data center energy demands skyrocketing, the shift toward silicon photonics is becoming an essential evolutionary step for scaling next-generation supercomputers.

Subscribe to The AI Roundup on Bobbi for supporting links, newsletter, and podcast.

text:
🔵 Blue light breakthrough in perovskite LEDs!

Scientists have achieved a major advance in blue perovskite LEDs using a clever molecular trick: isomeric multi-hydrogen-bonding. By engineering molecules that form multiple hydrogen bonds in specific geometric arrangements, researchers dramatically improved the stability and efficiency of blue-emitting perovskite devices — historically the weakest link in full-color perovskite display technology. This could be a game-changer for next-gen displays and solid-state lighting, potentially rivaling or surpassing OLED technology in both performance and manufacturing cost. Blue perovskite LEDs have long lagged behind their red and green counterparts — this breakthrough may finally close the gap and unlock commercial viability for the entire perovskite LED ecosystem.

#PerovskiteLED #DisplayTechnology #MaterialsScience #Photonics #Nanotechnology

Companies in this space: Samsung Electronics ($SSNLF, OTC), Universal Display Corporation ($OLED, NASDAQ), Merck KGaA ($MKKGY, OTC)

Which one is the most underrated?

text:
Good analysis from Anders Storm on what Computex showed about the next phase of AI optical infrastructure
It covers the move toward large optically connected compute clusters and the Wiwynn + Ayar rack scale demo, plus he tackles a few key questions floating around the sector on timelines and bottlenecks

Recommended if you track CPO / photonics

$SIVE $SIVEF $AAOI $LITE $COHR

text:
$POET is not just a small-cap semiconductor stock.

The market often overlooks POET Technologies as a niche name, but its exposure is highly asymmetric in the AI infrastructure buildout.

Its real exposure is:

1.Silicon photonics-based optical interconnects
2.Ultra-high-speed AI data center connectivity
3.Power-efficient chip-to-chip communication
4.Next-generation optical engine architecture
5.Scaling bottlenecks in AI compute infrastructure

The key question now:

Is $POET still an early-stage AI infrastructure winner, or already overextended after the repricing?

From my perspective, this is a high-risk, high-reward structural option on AI data movement — not a traditional semiconductor valuation story.

Key driver here is simple:

AI compute scaling is increasingly limited by data transfer, not just processing power.

If optical interconnect adoption accelerates, $POET sits in a potentially critical bottleneck solution layer.

But this is not a stable compounder.

It is a speculative infrastructure bet with execution and adoption risk still unresolved.

Position sizing and timing matter significantly here.

Do not treat it like a blue-chip AI name.

Treat it like an asymmetric infrastructure option on the future of data movement.

text:
The smartphone slowdown is a capital allocation story now. The headlines say Xiaomi, OPPO, and vivo are cutting their 2026 shipment targets.
The market will conclude that smartphone demand is weakening. I dont think so.
What's really happening is that AI is changing how the semiconductor industry allocates scarce resources.
Consumer electronics defined the semiconductor supply chain for many yrs.
If you were a memory supplier, the priority list was obvious:
Smartphones.
PCs.
Consumer devices.
Today, that hierarchy has changed.
The highest-value customers are no longer shipping the most devices. They're deploying the most compute.
HBM.
DDR5.
Enterprise SSDs.
Advanced packaging.
These products are serving AI infrastructure first, not smartphones. This is why the current memory shortage matters. It's not simply because supply is tight but because every wafer, every packaging slot, every engineering team, and every dollar of capital now has a higher-return destination. #ROI 
When an AI server can generate significantly higher value than a smartphone, the industry's resource allocation changes accordingly.
This is not just an engineering decision.
It's a capital allocation decision!!!🧐
And capital always follows the highest return.
That changes everything.
For smartphone OEMs, supply chain management used to mean negotiating better prices and securing long-term supply. Now it increasingly means competing for manufacturing priority.
That's a fundamental change.
The same pattern will likely extend beyond memory.
Advanced packaging.
High-end substrates.
Silicon photonics.
Power delivery.
Liquid cooling.
High-speed interconnects.
Engineering talent.
...
Each of these will become another allocation problem before it becomes a technology problem.
This is why I don't see Xiaomi, OPPO, and vivo's shipment cuts as isolated smartphone news.
I see them as evidence that AI is repricing the entire semiconductor supply chain.
They'll be the ones that consistently secure access to scarce manufacturing capacity, advanced packaging, critical materials, engineering talent, and long-term supply.
In semiconductors, shortages rarely begin as supply problems.
They begin as prioritization decisions.
Today it's smartphones.
Tomorrow it could be automotive, industrial, networking, or robotics.
AI isn't just increasing semiconductor demand.
It's changing who gets access to engineering talent, capital, manufacturing capacity, and ultimately, innovation itself. #SCM #SemiVon #Procurement

The headlines say Xiaomi, OPPO, and vivo are cutting their 2026 shipment targets. The market will conclude that smartphone demand is weakening. I dont think so. What's really happening is that AI is changing how the semiconductor industry allocates scarce resources. Consumer electronics defined the semiconductor supply chain for many yrs. If you were a memory supplier, the priority list was obvious: Smartphones. PCs. Consumer devices. Today, that hierarchy has changed. The highest-value customers are no longer shipping the most devices. They're deploying the most compute. HBM. DDR5. Enterprise SSDs. Advanced packaging. These products are serving AI infrastructure first, not smartphones. This is why the current memory shortage matters. It's not simply because supply is tight but because every wafer, every packaging slot, every engineering team, and every dollar of capital now has a higher-return destination. #ROI When an AI server can generate significantly higher value than a smartphone, the industry's resource allocation changes accordingly. This is not just an engineering decision. It's a capital allocation decision!!!🧐 And capital always follows the highest return. That changes everything. For smartphone OEMs, supply chain management used to mean negotiating better prices and securing long-term supply. Now it increasingly means competing for manufacturing priority. That's a fundamental change. The same pattern will likely extend beyond memory. Advanced packaging. High-end substrates. Silicon photonics. Power delivery. Liquid cooling. High-speed interconnects. Engineering talent. ... Each of these will become another allocation problem before it becomes a technology problem. This is why I don't see Xiaomi, OPPO, and vivo's shipment cuts as isolated smartphone news. I see them as evidence that AI is repricing the entire semiconductor supply chain. They'll be the ones that consistently secure access to scarce manufacturing capacity, advanced packaging, critical materials, engineering talent, and long-term supply. In semiconductors, shortages rarely begin as supply problems. They begin as prioritization decisions. Today it's smartphones. Tomorrow it could be automotive, industrial, networking, or robotics. AI isn't just increasing semiconductor demand. (2/3) $CIEN photonics! 

Almost every name in this group looks in big trouble to me.  My trouble I mean months of issues ahead. 

This particular name had a tremendous ~500% run.  Topped early June - bear flagged the entire month of June on top of the 100 day ma.  Then began to fall out of that flag Friday.  Below both the 21 and 50 ema, and 21 starting to curl aggressively down.  

Weekly - this let's us see why it was sitting on the 100 day moving average all month; it  basically aligns with the 10 week moving average (I use 9 rather than 10) 

This is "more promising" then the daily but only in the fact, it still is holding on by a fingernail.  If this breaks....

With its groundbreaking Eviyos smart headlight platform already securing over five hundred million euros in lifetime design wins, the company is positioning itself to capture massive growth through next-generation development agreements in artificial intelligence photonics and augmented reality smart glasses. Financially, a major balance sheet restructuring, including a successful bond refinancing and a lucrative sensor business sale, has dramatically reduced its interest burden and cut its pro-forma leverage ratio down to two and a half times. Trading at a discounted pro-forma enterprise value to EBITDA multiple of under six times, the market may still be underappreciating the profitability potential of this leaner, highly specialized turnaround candidate. Will these high-value growth catalysts and aggressive debt reduction be enough to finally reverse years of structural challenges and unlock substantial long-term value for patient shareholders?
